questions.question: Which unit is relative to the root element's font-size?
questions.explanation: rem (root em) is always relative to the root element's font-size, making it predictable and not affected by nesting.

questions.question: What issue can occur with nested em units?
questions.explanation: em compounds when nested — 0.8em inside another 0.8em results in 0.64em total. rem avoids this issue.

questions.question: Which unit represents 1% of the viewport width?
questions.explanation: vw (viewport width) equals 1% of the viewport width. 100vw fills the entire viewport width.

questions.question: What does fr stand for in CSS Grid?
questions.explanation: fr represents a fraction of the available space in a CSS Grid container, distributing only free space after gaps and fixed tracks.

questions.question: Which unit is best for setting a readable line length?
questions.explanation: ch (character unit) is based on the width of the '0' character. max-width: 60-70ch creates optimal line length for readability.

questions.question: What unit is commonly recommended for typography in responsive designs?
questions.explanation: rem is recommended for typography because it respects user font-size preferences, doesn't compound like em, and provides consistent sizing.

questions.question: Which unit solves the mobile browser 100vh issue with dynamic toolbars?
options: All of the above — each handles viewport differently
questions.explanation: dvh (dynamic), svh (small), and lvh (large) each handle mobile browser chrome differently: dynamic adjusts, small is smallest possible, large is largest.

questions.question: What does calc(100% - 80px) do?
options: Calculates the remaining width after subtracting 80px
questions.explanation: calc() allows mixing units in arithmetic. calc(100% - 80px) is commonly used for full-width layouts with fixed-width sidebars.

questions.question: What does the em unit for width reference?
questions.explanation: When used for width, em is relative to the element's own computed font-size, not its parent's font-size.

questions.question: What is the recommended safe value for vw when used alone for font-size?
questions.explanation: vw-sized text doesn't scale with browser zoom. Using clamp(min-rem, preferred-vw, max-rem) ensures accessibility and responsive sizing.

questions.question: What does the min(100%, 1200px) function do?
questions.explanation: min() selects the smallest value. min(100%, 1200px) creates a responsive container that fills the width up to 1200px.

questions.question: What is the default font-size of the root element in most browsers?
questions.explanation: The default root font-size is 16px in most browsers. Users can change this in browser settings, which is why relative units are important.

questions.question: Why is setting html { font-size: 10px } problematic?
questions.explanation: Setting a fixed pixel font-size overrides user preferences. Use font-size: 62.5% instead (10px at default 16px) to maintain accessibility.

questions.question: What does the ex unit represent?
options: The height of the 'x' character in the element's font
questions.explanation: ex is relative to the height of the 'x' character in the element's font. It's useful for vertical measurements in typography.

questions.question: What is the difference between fr and % in CSS Grid?
options: fr distributes only available space after gaps; % includes gaps in calculation
questions.explanation: fr distributes free space after accounting for gaps and fixed tracks. Using % for all columns can cause overflow when gaps are present.

questions.question: What does the clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) function achieve?
options: Font-size that scales between 1rem and 2rem based on viewport
questions.explanation: clamp() creates fluid typography: minimum 1rem, scales with 2.5vw as preferred, capped at 2rem maximum.

questions.question: When should you use px instead of relative units?
options: For borders, shadows, and fine details that shouldn't scale
questions.explanation: px is appropriate for borders (1px solid), box-shadows, media query breakpoints, and icon sizing — things that shouldn't scale with user preferences.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the lh unit?
questions.explanation: lh is relative to the line-height of the current element, useful for setting heights that match text line heights.

questions.question: What accessibility consideration applies to using vw for typography?
options: vw-based text doesn't scale with browser zoom
vw text is too large on mobile
vw is not supported in screen readers
questions.explanation: Browser zoom does not scale vw-based text proportionally, which can make text unreadable for users who rely on zoom for accessibility.

questions.question: What is the correct CSS for a responsive container that is 90% width up to 1200px?
questions.explanation: Both min(90%, 1200px) with margin-inline: auto and width: 90% with max-width: 1200px and margin: 0 auto achieve the same responsive container behavior.
